questions.text: During each cycle, alternating voltage reaches a peak value:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. While the voltage waveform transitions from positive to negative and back again, it only reaches its peak value twice per cycle, not once.

This is the correct answer. In an alternating voltage or current waveform, one complete cycle consists of a positive half-cycle and a negative half-cycle. During each cycle, the voltage reaches its peak value (maximum positive value) twice, and this occurs when the voltage waveform transitions from the negative half-cycle to the positive half-cycle or vice versa.
This option is also not correct. The voltage does not reach its peak value four times during a single cycle. It reaches the peak value only once per cycle.

options.text: A number of times depending on the frequency
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. The number of times the voltage reaches its peak value during each cycle is not determined by the frequency but by the nature of the alternating waveform. In standard AC waveforms, it reaches the peak value once per cycle. So, during each cycle of an alternating voltage, the voltage reaches its peak value one time.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In an alternating voltage or current waveform, one complete cycle consists of a positive half-cycle and a negative half-cycle. During each cycle, the voltage reaches its peak value (maximum positive value) twice, and this occurs when the voltage waveform transitions from the negative half-cycle to the positive half-cycle or vice versa.
hints: alternating voltage reaches a peak value

questions.text: The basic circuit elements of A.C circuit are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A resistor is a fundamental component of both A.C and D.C circuits. In A.C circuits, resistors provide opposition to the flow of alternating current, which leads to the dissipation of electrical energy in the form of heat. Resistor impedance (opposition to current) is the same at all frequencies, so it is a basic element in A.C circuits.

An inductor is another basic element of A.C circuits. Inductors store energy in the form of a magnetic field and resist changes in current flow. In A.C circuits, inductors introduce phase shifts between voltage and current and are used in applications such as filters and transformers.

Capacitors are essential components of A.C circuits. They store electrical energy in an electric field and release it when needed. Capacitors can also introduce phase shifts in A.C circuits and are used in applications like filters and energy storage devices.

This is the correct answer. All three elements—resistor, inductor, and capacitor—are basic and fundamental components of A.C circuits. They play distinct roles in A.C circuits, and their combined use allows for a wide range of circuit behavior and applications. In summary, the basic circuit elements of an A.C circuit are resistor, inductor, and capacitor, so the correct answer is d) All the three.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. All three elements—resistor, inductor, and capacitor—are basic and fundamental components of A.C circuits. They play distinct roles in A.C circuits, and their combined use allows for a wide range of circuit behavior and applications. In summary, the basic circuit elements of an A.C circuit are resistor, inductor, and capacitor, so the correct answer is d) All the three.

questions.text: In an A.C circuit with resistor only, the current and voltage have a phase angle of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. In an A.C circuit with only a resistor, the current and voltage are in phase, meaning they reach their peak values and change direction at the same time. There is no 90° phase angle between them.

This is the correct answer. In a purely resistive A.C circuit, the current and voltage are in phase with each other, meaning they are synchronized and have no phase difference. They reach their maximum values and change direction simultaneously.

This option is not correct. A 180° phase angle would indicate that the current and voltage are perfectly out of phase, with the current leading or lagging the voltage by half a cycle. In a purely resistive circuit, they are in phase, not 180° out of phase.

While some A.C circuits exhibit phase differences between current and voltage, in a circuit with only a resistor, the correct answer is 0°. There is indeed a phase angle of 0° between the current and voltage in this case.So, in an A.C circuit with a resistor only, the current and voltage have a phase angle of 0°, which means they are in phase with each other.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a purely resistive A.C circuit, the current and voltage are in phase with each other, meaning they are synchronized and have no phase difference. They reach their maximum values and change direction simultaneously.
hints: current and voltage have a phase angle

questions.text: In an A.C circuit with capacitor only, the current:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Leads the voltage by 90o or π/2
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a circuit with only a capacitor, the current leads the voltage by 90 degrees (π/2 radians) in phase. This phase relationship occurs because the voltage across a capacitor leads to a change in the rate of charge or discharge of the capacitor, causing the current to be out of phase with the voltage.

options.text: Lags behind voltage by 90o or π/2
options.explanation: This is not correct. In a pure capacitor circuit, the current leads the voltage by 90°, as explained in option (a). Lags behind means a 90° phase shift in the opposite direction, which is not the case.
This option is not correct. In a circuit with only a capacitor, the current and voltage are not in phase. As mentioned, the current leads the voltage by 90°.

The correct answer is not "None of the above." The correct answer is option (a), indicating that the current leads the voltage by 90° in a capacitor-only A.C circuit. So, in an A.C circuit with a capacitor only, the current leads the voltage by 90° or π/2 radians in phase.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a circuit with only a capacitor, the current leads the voltage by 90 degrees (π/2 radians) in phase. This phase relationship occurs because the voltage across a capacitor leads to a change in the rate of charge or discharge of the capacitor, causing the current to be out of phase with the voltage.

questions.text: At higher frequency of the alternating current, the capacitive reactance XC:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. Capacitive reactance (Xc) decreases as the frequency of alternating current increases. The relationship between capacitive reactance and frequency is inversely proportional. As the frequency goes up, the capacitive reactance goes dow

This is the correct answer. At higher frequencies, capacitive reactance decreases. The formula for capacitive reactance is Xc = 1 / (2πfC), where f is the frequency and C is the capacitance. As f increases, Xc decreases.
This option is not correct. Capacitive reactance changes with the frequency of the alternating current. It is not constant.

options.text: Increases only when the voltage increases
options.explanation: This option is not correct. The frequency of the alternating current is the primary factor affecting capacitive reactance. While voltage may affect the current in the circuit, it does not directly impact capacitive reactance. In summary, as the frequency of the alternating current increases, the capacitive reactance decreases, making option (b) the correct answer.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. At higher frequencies, capacitive reactance decreases. The formula for capacitive reactance is Xc = 1 / (2πfC), where f is the frequency and C is the capacitance. As f increases, Xc decreases.

questions.text: In an inductive A.C circuit, the current:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Lags behind the voltage by 90o or π/2
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In an inductive A.C circuit, the current lags behind the voltage by 90 degrees (π/2 radians) in phase. This phase relationship occurs because of the time it takes for the current to build up in an inductor when the voltage across it changes.

options.text: Leads the voltage by 90o or π/2
options.explanation: This option is not correct. In an inductive A.C circuit, the current lags behind the voltage, as explained in option (a). Leading the voltage by 90° would imply a different phase relationship, which is not characteristic of an inductive circuit.

options.text: Lags behind the voltage by 180o or π
options.explanation: This option is not correct. Lags behind by 180° would indicate that the current and voltage are in opposite phases, which is not the case in an inductive circuit. The correct phase lag is 90° or π/2.

options.text: Is in phase with the voltage
options.explanation: This option is not correct. In an inductive A.C circuit, the current and voltage are not in phase with each other. They have a phase difference of 90°, with the current lagging behind the voltage.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In an inductive A.C circuit, the current lags behind the voltage by 90 degrees (π/2 radians) in phase. This phase relationship occurs because of the time it takes for the current to build up in an inductor when the voltage across it changes.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. A 60° phase angle does not represent the relationship between the r.m.s value and the maximum value of alternating current.

This is the correct answer. In an alternating current waveform, the r.m.s value is equal to the maximum value of the current when the phase angle is 45°. This is a fundamental relationship in A.C circuit analysis. The r.m.s value represents the effective value of the current or voltage in an A.C circuit.
This option is also not correct. A 30° phase angle does not represent the relationship between the r.m.s value and the maximum value of alternating current.
This option is also not correct. A 90° phase angle does not represent the relationship between the r.m.s value and the maximum value of alternating current.

questions.text: A resonance curve for RLC series circuit is a plot of frequency versus:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Voltage: While voltage does play a role in the behavior of the circuit, the resonance curve primarily focuses on how the current amplitude (or magnitude) varies with frequency.

Current: The current is a critical parameter in an RLC series circuit, and the resonance curve illustrates how the current amplitude changes as the frequency of the applied alternating current varies. At the resonant frequency, the current is often at its maximum, which is a characteristic feature of resonance in such circuits.

Impedance: Impedance is the overall opposition to the flow of current in the circuit and is affected by the frequency. However, the resonance curve specifically shows the behavior of current concerning frequency, and impedance is related to this behavior.
Reactance: Reactance is a component of impedance, but the resonance curve doesn't directly plot reactance against frequency. It focuses on the broader behavior of current.

questions.explanation: Current: The current is a critical parameter in an RLC series circuit, and the resonance curve illustrates how the current amplitude changes as the frequency of the applied alternating current varies. At the resonant frequency, the current is often at its maximum, which is a characteristic feature of resonance in such circuits.

questions.text: The power factor of resonant series circuit is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a resonant series circuit, the power factor is equal to 1, which represents a perfect or unity power factor. This occurs because, at the resonant frequency, the inductive reactance and capacitive reactance cancel each other out, resulting in a net impedance that is purely resistive. As a result, the current and voltage are in phase, and there is no phase difference between them. This leads to a power factor of 1, indicating that all the power is real and is used to do useful work.
This option is not correct. A power factor of 0 indicates that all the power is reactive, which is not the case in a resonant series circuit at its resonant frequency.
This option is not correct. A power factor of -1 is not a physically meaningful value in the context of power factor analysis.

This option is not correct. A power factor of 0.5 represents a lagging power factor, indicating a phase difference between current and voltage. In a resonant series circuit, the power factor is unity (1), and there is no phase difference. In summary, the power factor of a resonant series circuit is 1, indicating a perfect or unity power factor with no phase difference between current and voltage.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a resonant series circuit, the power factor is equal to 1, which represents a perfect or unity power factor. This occurs because, at the resonant frequency, the inductive reactance and capacitive reactance cancel each other out, resulting in a net impedance that is purely resistive. As a result, the current and voltage are in phase, and there is no phase difference between them. This leads to a power factor of 1, indicating that all the power is real and is used to do useful work. P=VI cosθ Cosθ is called the power factor. Power is max when cosθ = 1
hints: power factor of resonant series circuit

questions.text: At resonance, the impedance of RLC series circuit is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. At resonance in an RLC series circuit, the impedance is primarily resistive and is equal to the resistance (R) of the circuit. This occurs because at the resonant frequency, the inductive reactance (XL) and capacitive reactance (XC) are equal in magnitude but cancel each other out. As a result, the impedance is purely resistive, and its value is equal to the resistance in the circuit.

This option is not correct. The inductive reactance (XL) is a component of the impedance in an RLC series circuit, but at resonance, it is balanced by the capacitive reactance (XC), resulting in a net impedance that is resistive, not inductive.

This option is not correct. Similar to XL, capacitive reactance (XC) is a component of the impedance in an RLC series circuit, but at resonance, it is balanced by the inductive reactance (XL), resulting in a resistive impedance.

The correct answer is not "None of the above." The impedance of an RLC series circuit is indeed equal to the resistance (R) of the circuit at resonance. In summary, at resonance in an RLC series circuit, the impedance is purely resistive and is equal to the resistance (R) of the circuit, as indicated in option a).

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. At resonance in an RLC series circuit, the impedance is primarily resistive and is equal to the resistance (R) of the circuit. This occurs because at the resonant frequency, the inductive reactance (XL) and capacitive reactance (XC) are equal in magnitude but cancel each other out. As a result, the impedance is purely resistive, and its value is equal to the resistance in the circuit.

questions.text: In series RC circuit when R=XC' then the phase angle is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. When R equals XC' in a series RC circuit, the phase angle is not 0 degrees. Instead, it's 45 degrees.

This option is not correct. A phase angle of 90 degrees typically occurs when there's a 90-degree phase difference between current and voltage, but it's not the case when R equals XC' in an RC circuit.
This option is not correct. A phase angle of 180 degrees implies that the current and voltage are in opposite phases, which is not the behavior in this specific scenario.

When the resistance (R) in a series RC circuit is equal to the capacitive reactance (XC'), it results in a special condition where the phase angle between the current and voltage is 45 degrees (π/4 radians). In this scenario, the circuit behaves as a half-power factor circuit, and the current and voltage are out of phase by 45 degrees. This condition occurs when the time constant of the RC circuit is equal to the period of the alternating current signal, resulting in a phase angle of 45 degrees. It's a distinctive situation where the capacitive and resistive components are balanced in such a way that the phase angle is fixed at 45 degrees.

questions.explanation: When the resistance (R) in a series RC circuit is equal to the capacitive reactance (XC'), it results in a special condition where the phase angle between the current and voltage is 45 degrees (π/4 radians). In this scenario, the circuit behaves as a half-power factor circuit, and the current and voltage are out of phase by 45 degrees. This condition occurs when the time constant of the RC circuit is equal to the period of the alternating current signal, resulting in a phase angle of 45 degrees. It's a distinctive situation where the capacitive and resistive components are balanced in such a way that the phase angle is fixed at 45 degrees.

questions.text: When electrons in the transmitting antenna vibrate 94000 times per second, they produce radiowaves having frequency:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. 9.4 kHz is an order of magnitude lower than the provided vibration frequency of 94,000 times per second, so it does not match the described scenario.
This option is not correct. 940 kHz is an order of magnitude lower than the provided vibration frequency of 94,000 times per second, so it does not match the described scenario.

This is the correct answer. When electrons in the transmitting antenna vibrate at a frequency of 94,000 times per second, the resulting radio waves have a frequency of 94 kHz. The unit "kHz" stands for kilohertz, which represents a frequency of 1,000 cycles per second. Therefore, 94 kHz corresponds to 94000 cycles per second, which is close to the given vibration frequency.

The correct answer is not "None of these." The correct frequency of the radio waves in this scenario is 94 kHz, as explained in option c. In summary, when electrons in a transmitting antenna vibrate at a rate of 94,000 times per second, the radio waves they produce have a frequency of 94 kHz (kilohertz).

questions.text: For common-emitter configuration of n-p-n transistor, the current gain β is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The current gain (β) for a common-emitter configuration of an n-p-n transistor is defined as the ratio of the collector current (IC) to the base current (IB). It represents how much the collector current is amplified with respect to the base current.
This option is not correct. IC/IE is not the definition of β. β specifically relates the collector and base currents.
This option is not correct. IE/IB is not the definition of β. β relates the collector current to the base current, not the emitter current.

This option is not correct. IB/IE is the current gain of the transistor's input section, but it is not represented by β. β is specifically related to the collector and base currents. In summary, the correct expression for the current gain (β) in the common-emitter configuration of an n-p-n transistor is IC/IB, as indicated in option a).

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. The current gain (β) for a common-emitter configuration of an n-p-n transistor is defined as the ratio of the collector current (IC) to the base current (IB). It represents how much the collector current is amplified with respect to the base current.

questions.text: An open loop OP-amplifier has output voltage V0 and difference between non-inverting input voltage Vi, its open loop gain is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In an open-loop configuration, the open-loop gain (AOL) of an op-amp is defined as the ratio of the output voltage (V0) to the input voltage (Vi). It quantifies how much the op-amp amplifies the input voltage to produce the output voltage.
This option is not correct. The open-loop gain is a ratio, not a product of the output and input voltages.
This option is not correct. The open-loop gain is defined as the output voltage divided by the input voltage, so it is AOL = V0/Vi, not the inverse Vi/V0.

The correct answer is not "None of these." The open-loop gain (AOL) is indeed defined as V0/Vi in an open-loop operational amplifier. In summary, the open-loop gain (AOL) of an open-loop operational amplifier is correctly represented by AOL = V0/Vi, as indicated in option a).

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In an open-loop configuration, the open-loop gain (AOL) of an op-amp is defined as the ratio of the output voltage (V0) to the input voltage (Vi). It quantifies how much the op-amp amplifies the input voltage to produce the output voltage.
hints: difference between non-inverting input voltage V

questions.text: A P-N junction or semiconductor diode cannot be used as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A P-N junction diode is commonly used as a rectifier to convert alternating current (AC) into direct current (DC). It allows current to flow in one direction while blocking it in the reverse direction, making it an essential component in rectifier circuits.

A P-N junction diode can be used as a detector in radio and communication circuits, where it demodulates amplitude-modulated (AM) signals to recover the original information. It is a fundamental part of diode detectors.

While a P-N junction diode by itself is not typically used as an oscillator, it can be integrated into various oscillator circuits as part of a larger circuit. Diodes can be used in relaxation oscillators, but they are not the primary component responsible for generating the oscillations.

A P-N junction diode is not suitable for amplification. It does not provide gain or amplification of input signals. For signal amplification, active components like transistors are used, not diodes. In summary, a P-N junction or semiconductor diode can be used as a rectifier, a detector, and can be part of oscillator circuits, but it cannot function as an amplifier, as it does not provide signal amplification. Amplification typically requires active components like transistors.

questions.explanation: A P-N junction diode is not suitable for amplification. It does not provide gain or amplification of input signals. For signal amplification, active components like transistors are used, not diodes. In summary, a P-N junction or semiconductor diode can be used as a rectifier, a detector, and can be part of oscillator circuits, but it cannot function as an amplifier, as it does not provide signal amplification. Amplification typically requires active components like transistors.
hints: P-N junction or semiconductor diode cannot be used

questions.text: The output of 2-input OR gates is 0 only when its:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In an OR gate, the output is 0 only when both of its inputs are 0. If both inputs are 1, or if either input is 1, the output will be 1. The OR gate performs a logical OR operation, and it produces a 1 (high) output when at least one of the inputs is 1. If both inputs are 0, the output will be 0 (low).
This option is not correct. In an OR gate, the output is not 0 when either input is 1. It is 1 in this case.
This option is not correct. In an OR gate, the output is 1 when both inputs are 1, not 0.

questions.text: An XOR gate produces an output only when its two inputs are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. An XOR gate can produce an output even when its inputs are high. The crucial factor for an XOR gate is whether the inputs are different or the same, not their absolute voltage levels.
This option is not correct. Similar to the previous case, an XOR gate can produce an output when its inputs are low, as long as they are different.

This is the correct answer. An XOR gate is designed to produce an output when its two inputs are different. If one input is high and the other is low, or vice versa, the XOR gate will produce a high output (1). If the inputs are the same (both high or both low), the XOR gate will produce a low output (0).

This option is not correct. An XOR gate does not produce an output when its inputs are the same. If both inputs are high or both inputs are low, the XOR gate's output will be low (0). In summary, an XOR gate produces an output only when its two inputs are different, as explained in option c).

questions.text: A NOR gate is ON only when its inputs are:

options.explanation: This option is not correct. A NOR gate is not ON when its inputs are ON. It is designed to produce an ON (high or 1) output when its inputs are both LOW (0).
This option is not correct. A NOR gate is not ON when its inputs are high. It produces an ON output when the inputs are low.
This option is not correct. A NOR gate is not ON when its inputs are high. It produces an ON output when the inputs are low.
This is the correct answer. A NOR gate is ON, meaning it produces an output of 1, when its inputs are both LOW (0). If either or both of its inputs are low, the NOR gate's output will be high (1).

questions.text: In alternating current the average value of current in cycle is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an alternating current (AC) circuit, the flow of electric current periodically changes direction. The current oscillates back and forth, completing one full cycle when it returns to its original direction. The average value of the current over one complete cycle is zero. The average value of the current in an AC circuit over one complete cycle is zero due to the continuous oscillation and symmetrical nature of the alternating current waveform. Average current is given by the formula:Iavg= 1/T∫0Ti(t)dtIavg is the average current,T is the total time period,i(t) is the instantaneous current at time tThe integral represents the area under the current vs. time curve over one complete cycle.

questions.explanation: In an alternating current (AC) circuit, the flow of electric current periodically changes direction. The current oscillates back and forth, completing one full cycle when it returns to its original direction. The average value of the current over one complete cycle is zero. The average value of the current in an AC circuit over one complete cycle is zero due to the continuous oscillation and symmetrical nature of the alternating current waveform. Average current is given by the formula:Iavg= 1/T∫0Ti(t)dtIavg is the average current,T is the total time period,i(t) is the instantaneous current at time tThe integral represents the area under the current vs. time curve over one complete cycle.
